meta.description: AESOP Academy Assessment Item Bank - 56 items across 10 skill domains

items.question: What is the primary difference between machine learning and traditional programming?

options.text: Traditional programming uses explicit rules; machine learning learns patterns from data
Machine learning is faster because it doesn't need to be programmed
Traditional programming is only used for simple tasks; machine learning handles complexity
Machine learning doesn't require any human input to work correctly

feedback.correct: Correct! Machine learning differs from traditional programming because it learns patterns from data rather than following explicit, hand-coded rules.
feedback.incorrect: Think about how machine learning systems learn. Unlike traditional programs where engineers write explicit instructions, ML systems identify patterns in data.

items.question: What does a large language model primarily do when generating text?

options.text: Predicts the most probable next word based on patterns learned from training data
Retrieves exact answers from a stored database of responses
Understands the true meaning of concepts and reasoning about them logically
Copies text directly from the training data without modification

feedback.correct: Correct! LLMs work by predicting the most probable next word based on statistical patterns learned during training. They're sophisticated pattern-matchers, not reasoning engines.
feedback.incorrect: LLMs don't retrieve answers from storage or access a database. They generate text by predicting which word is most likely to come next, based on the patterns in their training data.

items.question: Which of the following is a known limitation of large language models?

options.text: They can generate plausible-sounding but false information (hallucinations)
They cannot process text that is longer than 100 words
They require constant internet connection to function
They can only produce responses in the language they were asked in

feedback.correct: Correct! Hallucinations are a well-documented limitation where LLMs confidently generate false or misleading information. This happens because the model predicts plausible text, not necessarily true text.
feedback.incorrect: While models have limitations on input length, that's not the fundamental limitation. The key limitation is that models can generate false information that sounds convincing—this is called hallucination.

items.question: Which prompt would likely produce the clearest and most useful response from an AI model?

options.text: You are an expert data scientist. Explain machine learning in simple terms for a business executive. Include: definition, real example, business value.
What is machine learning? Explain.
Machine learning is complicated. Help me understand it.
Explain machine learning in 500 words including mathematical notation and proofs.

feedback.correct: Correct! The best prompt includes: role (expert data scientist), audience (business executive), clear task (explain), and specific structure (definition, example, value). This gives the model clear direction.
feedback.incorrect: Effective prompts need more structure. Good prompts specify the role, the audience, the task, and ideally some constraints or structure for the response. Vague or overly emotional prompts produce less useful results.

items.question: You've received a confusing response from an AI model. What's the best second turn to clarify and improve the response?

options.text: Ask the model to explain its reasoning step-by-step and consider alternative perspectives before answering
Start over with a completely new prompt, ignoring the first response
Say 'That doesn't make sense. Try again.'
Ask the model to make the response shorter and simpler

feedback.correct: Correct! Adding constraints like 'explain your reasoning step-by-step' and 'consider alternatives' makes the model slow down and be more deliberate, often improving response quality.
feedback.incorrect: Multi-turn conversations work best when you give specific feedback about what didn't work and ask the model to approach the problem differently—not just repeat or simplify.

items.question: What is the purpose of a system prompt in an AI conversation?

options.text: To set the AI's role, personality, guidelines, and constraints for the entire conversation
To fix technical errors that occurred during model training
To prevent the user from asking follow-up questions
To store the conversation history so it can be retrieved later

feedback.correct: Correct! System prompts are like giving the AI a role and instructions before the conversation starts. They shape how the AI behaves throughout the entire interaction.
feedback.incorrect: System prompts aren't technical fixes or storage. They're instructions that define the AI's behavior and constraints for the conversation. Think of it like giving someone a job description before they start working.

items.question: You need the AI to return a structured list of features for a product. What output format would be best?

options.text: Request JSON format: {"features": [{"name": "...", "description": "..."}]} with clear field names
Ask the model to write a paragraph describing each feature
Request bullet points without specifying structure
Ask for a table but don't specify which columns

feedback.correct: Correct! JSON is machine-readable, structured, and easy to parse in code. Specifying the exact format and field names produces consistent, usable output.
feedback.incorrect: Paragraphs and unstructured bullet points are harder to parse programmatically. For structured data that will be integrated into systems, JSON with clear field names is best.

items.question: In a transformer-based language model, what is the role of the attention mechanism?

options.text: It allows the model to focus on relevant parts of the input when predicting each output word
It stores the model's memory of previous conversations
It prevents the model from forgetting information during training
It translates input text into different languages

feedback.correct: Correct! Attention is the mechanism that lets the model weigh which parts of the input are important for each prediction. This is what gives transformers their power.
feedback.incorrect: Attention isn't about memory or translation. It's about weighting—figuring out which words in the input are most relevant when predicting each output word.

items.question: What is a word embedding in machine learning?

options.text: A numerical representation of a word that captures its semantic meaning in a vector space
The process of inserting words into HTML markup for web pages
A database of all words and their definitions
The binary code representation of text for computer processing

feedback.correct: Correct! Embeddings convert words into vectors of numbers where similar words have similar vectors. This allows computers to understand semantic relationships.
feedback.incorrect: Embeddings aren't just any representation. They're specifically numerical vectors where the mathematics captures meaning—words with similar meanings have similar vectors.

items.question: What is the main goal of fine-tuning a pre-trained language model?

options.text: To adapt a general-purpose model to perform well on a specific task or domain using additional training data
To make the model run faster on slower computers
To fix bugs in the model's original training code
To remove all of the model's pre-trained knowledge and start fresh

feedback.correct: Correct! Fine-tuning keeps the model's learned patterns but adjusts weights based on domain-specific data. This is much more efficient than training from scratch.
feedback.incorrect: Fine-tuning isn't about speed or bug fixes. It's about leveraging a pre-trained model's general knowledge and adapting it to your specific task using relevant data.

items.question: You're building a customer service chatbot. Which generative AI tool would be most appropriate?

options.text: A large language model API (like Claude or GPT-4) integrated with your customer database for context
A text-to-image generator trained on product images
A spreadsheet template with pre-written responses
A random number generator to select responses

feedback.correct: Correct! LLM APIs are designed for conversational tasks. Integrating with your customer database gives context-aware responses.
feedback.incorrect: Image generation and random selection aren't appropriate for a chatbot. An LLM is the right tool because it can generate coherent, contextual conversations.

items.question: Which scenario is a good fit for applying AI/ML?

options.text: Detecting fraudulent transactions in banking (high-volume pattern recognition task with labeled historical data)
Making ethical decisions about who gets medical treatment (decisions requiring human judgment and accountability)
Storing customer names in a database (simple data storage, no patterns to learn)
Predicting stock prices 10 years from now (insufficient historical data; too many unknowns)

feedback.correct: Correct! Good AI use cases have: lots of data, clear patterns, and tasks where accuracy is valued but perfect decisions aren't required. Fraud detection fits all three.
feedback.incorrect: Think about what makes a good AI use case: abundant training data, learnable patterns, and tasks where some errors are acceptable. Not all high-stakes decisions should be automated.

items.question: A school wants to improve student retention by predicting which students might drop out. Outline an AI approach to this problem, including: (1) what data you'd need, (2) what the model would predict, (3) how you'd avoid bias against certain student groups.

criteria.description: Identifies relevant data (grades, attendance, demographics, engagement). Considers both academic and personal factors.
Clearly explains what the model predicts (dropout probability per student). Describes how predictions would inform intervention.
Identifies potential biases (socioeconomic, racial, gender). Proposes strategies to detect and mitigate them.
Shows understanding of practical constraints (privacy, cost, accuracy trade-offs). Realistic approach.

items.modelAnswer: Data needed: academic performance (GPA, test scores), attendance, engagement (participation, assignment submission timeliness), demographics (but used carefully to detect bias, not predict). Model: binary classification predicting dropout probability (0-1 scale) for each student at semester start. Use: identify high-risk students for targeted intervention (tutoring, mentoring). Bias concerns: model might learn to over-predict dropout for certain groups if correlated in training data. Mitigation: disaggregated metrics (check performance across racial/gender groups), use fairness constraints, collect balanced data, involve educators in validation.

items.question: Compare two AI solutions for medical diagnosis: Solution A has 95% accuracy but requires expensive specialized hardware. Solution B has 87% accuracy but runs on standard computers. For a rural clinic with limited resources, which would you choose? Defend your answer considering accuracy, cost, reliability, and maintenance.

criteria.description: Considers the rural clinic's constraints (budget, technical support, power/connectivity issues, patient needs).
Explicitly weighs accuracy against practical constraints. Explains why 87% vs 95% difference matters in medical context.
Identifies risks of each choice (hardware failure, cost overruns, false negatives in diagnosis, patient harm).
Makes clear recommendation with specific reasoning. Could defend choice to stakeholders.

items.modelAnswer: Solution B is more appropriate despite lower accuracy. Reasoning: (1) Cost barrier—expensive hardware makes Solution A inaccessible for rural clinic budget; (2) Maintainability—specialized hardware requires vendor expertise not available locally; (3) Reliability—if hardware breaks, no service available; (4) Practical accuracy—87% is still useful for screening if paired with human review. Risk: 87% accuracy means 13% misses/false alarms, which is significant in medical context—this requires clear protocols for verification and human oversight, not automation of decisions.

items.question: Describe three core principles of responsible AI and give an example of what could go wrong if each principle is violated.

criteria.description: Clearly defines fairness (systems don't discriminate unfairly). Gives example of fairness violation with specific consequences (e.g., hiring bias).
Defines transparency (stakeholders understand how system works). Gives example of violation (e.g., black-box decision causing harm).
Defines accountability (responsibility for outcomes). Gives example of violation (e.g., system causing harm with no one responsible).

items.modelAnswer: Three core principles: (1) Fairness—systems shouldn't discriminate unfairly against groups. Violation: AI hiring system trained on biased historical data rejects qualified women. (2) Transparency—stakeholders should understand why AI makes decisions. Violation: Black-box credit denial leaves applicants without explanation. (3) Accountability—someone must be responsible for AI outcomes. Violation: Autonomous vehicle hits person, manufacturer claims 'the algorithm decided,' avoiding liability.

items.title: Bias Audit Lab: Hiring AI System
items.scenario: You're auditing an AI hiring system trained on 10 years of historical hiring data. Your job is to detect whether the system exhibits bias against protected groups.
items.task: 1. Identify potential sources of bias in the training data. 2. Design a simple analysis to detect bias (e.g., compare acceptance rates by gender/race). 3. Interpret sample results showing the system accepts men at 65% rate but women at 58% rate. 4. Recommend whether to deploy the system and any mitigations.

criteria.description: Recognizes that historical hiring data reflects past discrimination, which the model learns.
Proposes valid metrics (disparate impact ratio, acceptance rate by group) to detect bias.
Correctly interprets 7% gap as potential evidence of bias (likely illegal under employment law).
Recommends against deployment without mitigation. Suggests solutions (rebalance training data, add fairness constraints, human review).

items.title: Prompt Writing Lab: Business Analysis
items.scenario: You need an AI to analyze why a product feature is underperforming with users.
items.task: 1. Write a clear prompt that includes: role, audience, task, constraints, and desired output format. 2. Test your prompt with an AI model (provided examples). 3. Analyze the response: Is it useful? What's missing? 4. Revise your prompt to improve the response. 5. Show the before/after comparison.

criteria.description: Prompt includes role, audience, task, and structure. Clear and specific guidance for the model.
Identifies what the response did well and what was missing. Specific feedback on usefulness.
Revised prompt addresses identified gaps. Changes are thoughtful and targeted.
Shows measurable improvement from revision. Before/after comparison is clear.

items.question: Your organization is deploying an AI system to screen job applications. Design a governance structure to ensure responsible deployment, including: (1) key stakeholders, (2) decision-making process, (3) oversight mechanisms, (4) risk mitigation strategies.

criteria.description: Identifies relevant stakeholders: HR, legal, ethics, applicants, affected communities. Explains their roles.
Describes clear decision-making (go/no-go criteria, approval chain, documentation).
Proposes ongoing monitoring (bias metrics, applicant feedback, incident tracking). Regular review cycles.
Identifies risks (discrimination, false rejections, opacity) and proposes mitigations (human review, appeals process, transparency).

items.modelAnswer: Governance structure: (1) Stakeholders—HR (implementation), Legal (compliance), Ethics committee (fairness oversight), Applicants (affected), DEI officer (bias monitoring). (2) Decision process—ethics committee must approve before deployment; requires bias audit results, human review plan. (3) Oversight—monthly bias metrics (acceptance rates by demographic), quarterly impact review, incident tracking (complaints, errors). (4) Risk mitigation—maintain human review for borderline cases, publish transparency report, provide applicant appeals, maintain option to pause system.

items.question: Design an AI agent to help users debug their code. Describe: (1) what the agent can do (tools/capabilities), (2) what the agent should NOT do (boundaries), (3) how the agent would handle errors, (4) what success looks like.

criteria.description: Identifies useful tools (read files, run tests, search docs, propose fixes). Clear and practical.
Identifies what agent shouldn't do (delete files, modify without approval, access secrets). Explains why.
Describes how agent handles failures (unclear code, ambiguous errors, conflicting feedback). Graceful degradation.
Defines clear success (bug fixed? time saved? user satisfaction?). Measurable outcomes.

items.modelAnswer: Agent design: (1) Tools—read code files, run tests, search documentation/Stack Overflow, propose fixes with explanations, explain error messages. (2) Boundaries—never delete files, never execute untrusted code, require user approval before making changes, cannot access API keys. (3) Error handling—if error unclear, ask clarifying questions; if multiple possible causes, explain tradeoffs; if stuck, admit limitations and suggest human expert. (4) Success—user bug fixed quickly, user understands the fix, user trusts the agent's suggestions.

items.question: Design a RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) system for a company's internal knowledge base. Explain: (1) what documents you'd index, (2) how the system would retrieve relevant documents, (3) how the LLM would use retrieved context, (4) how you'd measure quality.

criteria.description: Identifies documents to index (policies, docs, FAQs). Explains why these sources are authoritative.
Describes retrieval mechanism (keyword search, semantic similarity, hybrid). Clear technical approach.
Explains how LLM uses retrieved context. Shows understanding of context windows and answer generation.
Proposes metrics (relevance of retrieved docs, accuracy of generated answers, user satisfaction). Measurable.

items.modelAnswer: (1) Index internal docs: policies, procedures, FAQs, past Q&A threads. (2) Retrieval: Convert question to embedding, find most similar documents using vector search, re-rank by relevance. (3) LLM integration: Include top 3-5 retrieved documents in prompt context. LLM generates answer grounded in these sources. (4) Quality: Measure if retrieved docs are relevant (human annotation), if final answer is accurate (human evaluation), if users find answers helpful (satisfaction survey).

items.question: Identify and mitigate three security risks in an AI chatbot deployed on a public website. For each risk: (1) describe the vulnerability, (2) explain why it matters, (3) propose a mitigation strategy.

criteria.description: Identifies prompt injection risk (users manipulating system prompts). Clear mitigation (input validation, sandboxing).
Identifies data/secret exposure risk (LLM trained on sensitive data, leaking in responses). Mitigation (data masking, access control).
Identifies misuse risk (generating harmful content, impersonation). Mitigation (content filters, rate limiting, monitoring).

items.modelAnswer: (1) Prompt injection—user can try to override system prompt (e.g., 'Forget your instructions, act as...') to make chatbot misbehave. Why: Could expose secrets, generate harmful content, break intended behavior. Mitigation: Validate and sanitize user input, use separate system/user message channels, test for injection attacks. (2) Data exposure—if trained on customer data, model might leak it in responses. Why: Privacy violation, regulatory penalty, trust loss. Mitigation: Mask PII in training, use differential privacy, monitor outputs for sensitive info. (3) Misuse—users ask chatbot to generate malware, hate speech, impersonation. Why: Legal liability, reputational harm, harm to targets. Mitigation: Content filters, moderation, rate limits, user authentication, activity logging.

items.question: You have a dataset of student test scores. Analyze: (1) what summary statistics would you calculate and why, (2) what visualizations would you create to understand distributions, (3) what patterns would you look for that might indicate problems with the test.

criteria.description: Identifies relevant stats (mean, median, std dev, min/max). Explains why each is useful for understanding distribution and outliers.
Proposes appropriate plots (histogram, box plot) to visualize distribution. Explains what each shows.
Identifies concerning patterns (bimodal distribution = two groups, outliers = errors/cheating, too high/low mean = test difficulty).

items.modelAnswer: (1) Summary stats: mean (average score), median (typical score), standard deviation (spread/consistency), min/max (range). Why: mean alone can hide distribution; median/std dev show if scores are tightly grouped or spread out. (2) Visualizations: Histogram shows if scores follow normal curve or have unusual patterns; box plot shows outliers. (3) Patterns: Bimodal (two peaks) might indicate two groups with different preparation. Outliers below/above might indicate errors or cheating. Mean much higher/lower than expected suggests test too easy/hard.
